questions.scenario: A proposed change moves receipt PDFs from Blob Storage into Azure SQL because the app already opens a database connection. The receipts are downloaded by customers, kept for audit, and never joined to order line items in queries. What should the reviewer ask first?

options.text: Whether the PDF files need database transactions with order line items, or whether durable object storage is still the better fit.
Whether the SQL server has enough CPU to accept larger binary values during checkout.
Whether the team can add a larger database tier before the next traffic peak.
Whether the app team prefers SQL backup tooling over storage lifecycle policy.

questions.explanation: The first question is about behavior. A file that users download usually belongs in object storage unless the application has a strong transactional reason to keep it inside the database.

questions.scenario: The team sees old retry keys in Cosmos DB and wants to delete the whole container. The container is named `idempotency-keys`, uses partition key `/idempotencyKey`, and has a TTL of seven days. What is the safer review conclusion?

options.text: The container is probably the wrong service because TTL means the data is temporary.
The TTL suggests the container was designed for short-lived keys, so inspect the expiry behavior before deleting resources.
The team should move keys into Blob Storage so old keys can be found by prefix.
The team should disable TTL to keep every key for support investigations.

questions.explanation: Short-lived known-key data can be a good Cosmos DB fit. TTL is part of the design, so the review should inspect the setting and the failure symptom before deleting the container.
